title: Bitrate Estimator Guide
summary: Understand how resolution, frame rate, codec, scene complexity, and quality target shape camera bitrate before storage sizing.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates a practical per-camera bitrate from image size, frame rate, codec, scene behavior, and quality expectations.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it before storage and retention planning when camera settings are not locked yet or when a design needs a defensible bitrate assumption.
bullets: Early video-storage design planning.
Expansion review or recorder replacement.
Existing-system cleanup, validation, or troubleshooting.
Explaining storage, retention, and quality tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical video-storage planning terms.

items.label: Resolution
items.description: The selected image size or video resolution used for the stream estimate.
items.whyItMatters: Resolution is one of the strongest bitrate drivers because larger frames carry more visual information.
items.commonMistake: Using a camera maximum resolution because it sounds better without checking whether storage and network capacity can support it.

links.label: Carry bitrate into storage sizing
links.note: Use the estimated bitrate as the downstream storage input.

items.label: Custom Width (px)
items.description: The horizontal pixel count used when Custom resolution is selected.
items.whyItMatters: Width combines with height to determine the total pixel load per frame.
items.commonMistake: Entering custom dimensions that do not match the actual stream profile or camera setting.

items.label: Custom Height (px)
items.description: The vertical pixel count used when Custom resolution is selected.
items.whyItMatters: Height completes the custom pixel count and affects the bitrate estimate directly.
items.commonMistake: Mixing width from one stream profile with height from another stream profile.

items.label: Frame Rate (fps)
items.description: The number of frames captured or encoded each second.
items.whyItMatters: Higher frame rates usually increase bitrate and storage demand because more frames are encoded every second.
items.commonMistake: Leaving frame rate at a high default even when the scene does not require it.

items.label: Codec
items.description: The compression codec assumed for the stream.
items.whyItMatters: Codec choice can materially change storage demand at the same resolution and frame rate.
items.commonMistake: Assuming two codecs produce the same file size or that newer codecs always behave perfectly on every VMS/client path.

links.label: Compare codec savings
links.note: Use this when evaluating a codec migration.

items.label: Scene Complexity
items.description: How difficult the scene is to compress based on motion, detail, lighting noise, and visual change.
examples: Parking lot with trees and headlights
items.whyItMatters: Complex scenes can push bitrate higher than clean lab assumptions.
items.commonMistake: Sizing from a calm test scene when the real scene has motion, weather, lighting changes, or visual noise.

items.label: Quality Target
items.description: The desired balance between compression and retained image quality.
items.whyItMatters: Higher quality settings preserve more detail but generally increase bitrate and storage demand.
items.commonMistake: Lowering quality only to save storage without checking whether the footage still meets the viewing or evidence need.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the stream assumption is reasonable for downstream planning. Watch means bitrate pressure should be reviewed. Risk means resolution, frame rate, codec, scene complexity, or quality target may create a heavy storage/network burden.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: add capacity, reduce demand, adjust policy, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most video-storage planning problems come from treating bitrate, storage, retention, RAID, and failure behavior as separate decisions even though they shape the same final retention promise.
bullets: Sizing storage from one average bitrate without checking peak, motion, codec, and retention behavior.
Treating nominal capacity as usable capacity after RAID, filesystem, spare, and operational reserves.
Ignoring growth, camera additions, quality changes, and retention policy changes after day one.
Failing to document assumptions before the recorder, storage tier, or archive policy is selected.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, VMS platform limits, camera-specific behavior, storage vendor guidance, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Camera bitrate can vary significantly with scene motion, lighting, compression settings, and codec behavior.
Usable storage should account for redundancy, reserved space, filesystem overhead, and operational margin.
Retention targets should be validated against stakeholder expectations, evidence needs, and system operation.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, expand, and troubleshoot.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal design review, manufacturer validation, field testing, or project-specific responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual VMS, recorder, camera model, codec profile, storage platform, and network path being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for retention, archive access, failure behavior, and acceptable image quality.
Document assumptions before implementation so future changes can be evaluated against the original design intent.
